title: Your Weakest Supplier
subtitle: Why other people's risk becomes yours

hook.metaphor: A chain is only as strong as its weakest link — and your organisation is a chain of suppliers. Your cloud host, your payroll provider, your delivery partner, the tiny startup that runs one critical feature. Each one you depend on can drag you down if *they* fail. Third-party risk is the simple, uncomfortable truth that you inherit the weaknesses of everyone you rely on.
hook.scenario: A bank with world-class security suffers a massive data breach. The hole wasn't in the bank — it was in a small contractor with access to the bank's systems and far weaker defences. The attackers walked in through the supplier. Whose breach is it, really?

learn: **Third-party (or vendor) risk** is the risk that an external party you depend on — a supplier, partner, contractor, or service provider — causes you harm by failing, being breached, or behaving badly. Modern businesses outsource enormously, so this risk has exploded.

The hardest truth: **you can outsource the activity, but not the responsibility.** When the contractor's weak security let attackers into the bank (our scenario), it was the *bank's* breach in the eyes of customers and regulators. 'Our vendor did it' is never an acceptable answer.

Third-party risk is rarely just one risk — it's a carrier for all the others. A vendor can expose you to **cyber risk** (their breach becomes yours), **operational risk** (their outage stops you), **compliance risk** (they mishandle data you're responsible for), and **reputational risk** (their scandal taints you).

And it goes deeper than you can see: your suppliers have *their* suppliers (**fourth parties** and beyond). A failure four links down the chain can still reach you — the dependency you didn't even know you had is often the one that bites.

This is squarely on the regulators' radar: Australia's **APRA CPS 230** explicitly requires managing material service-provider risk, the EU's **DORA** governs ICT third parties, and guidance worldwide now demands firms understand and control their supply chains.
bridge: So at work, you can stop thinking of suppliers as 'someone else's problem'. For anyone you depend on, ask: *what would happen to us if they were breached, went down, or behaved badly — and would we still be on the hook?* (You would be.) Your risk perimeter isn't your own walls; it's the walls of everyone you rely on.

interactions.title: Whose breach is it?
interactions.prompt: Attackers entered a secure bank through a weak contractor with system access. How should the bank see it?

options.label: The contractor's problem — not the bank's fault.
options.feedback: Customers and regulators hold the bank responsible. You can outsource the work, never the accountability. 'Our vendor did it' won't save you.

options.label: The bank's breach — it owns the risk of everyone it gives access to.
options.feedback: Exactly. Your risk perimeter extends to every party you depend on or give access to. A weak supplier is your weak link, and the consequences land on you.

options.label: Nobody's fault — these things just happen.
options.feedback: Giving a weakly-defended contractor access to critical systems is a known, manageable risk. This was a third-party risk failure, not bad luck.

interactions.title: What does the vendor expose you to?
interactions.prompt: Sort each supplier failure by the type of risk it creates for you.

items.label: Your cloud vendor is hacked and your data leaks
Your payments provider goes offline for a day
A vendor mishandles personal data you're responsible for
A supplier's ransomware spreads into your systems
A key logistics partner collapses mid-contract

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Third-party risk is inheriting the weaknesses of everyone you depend on — and since you can outsource the work but never the responsibility, your weakest supplier is your weakest link.”

checkpoint.q: The core principle of third-party risk is…
options: Vendors are always someone else's problem
You can outsource the activity but not the responsibility
checkpoint.explain: Accountability stays with you even when the work — and the failure — happens at a vendor.

options: Your supplier's supplier — a deeper dependency you may not see
checkpoint.explain: Risk can reach you from suppliers of your suppliers — dependencies further down the chain.

checkpoint.q: Why has third-party risk grown so much?
options: Companies outsource far more than they used to
checkpoint.explain: Heavy reliance on cloud, SaaS, and outsourced services means more of your risk lives outside your walls.
